title: Cheese and Onion Pasty
subtitle: Sharp cheddar, thinly sliced potato, and raw onion in shortcrust, crimped at the top
excerpt: A cheese and onion pasty uses the same raw-fill method as a Cornish pasty: thinly sliced raw potato and onion, and good cheddar, enclosed in shortcrust and baked. The steam inside cooks the vegetables and melts the cheese into the potato.
sourceNotes: Method follows the Cornish pasty raw-fill technique documented across British regional cookbooks. The cheese and onion filling is a vegetarian variant long established in British bakery tradition, particularly in the Midlands and north of England.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked assembled pasties on a lined tray until solid, then bag. Bake from frozen at 180°C for 65 to 70 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the quantities to make 8 pasties. Freeze half unbaked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble and refrigerate for up to 4 hours before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: A crumbly, rich pastry made by rubbing cold fat into flour until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs, then binding with water. It is firm enough to hold a filling but more tender than hot water crust. Used for both sweet and savoury pies and pasties.

content.text: Cheese and onion pasty uses raw potato, raw onion, and grated cheddar layered into shortcrust pastry and baked together. The raw-fill method means the potato slices cook in the steam from the vegetables and cheese: they finish soft and moist with no sogginess. The key is slicing the potato very thinly so it cooks through in the time the pastry takes to bake.

items.prepNote: peeled and very thinly sliced, no more than 2 mm

content.text: Rub the cold lard and butter into the flour and salt with your fingertips until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. This forms the base of the
. Add 5 to 6 tablespoons of cold water, one at a time, and bring together into a firm dough. Do not overwork. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes.
Heat the oven to 200°C. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Divide the rested pastry into 4 equal portions. Roll each into a circle about 25 cm across and 3 mm thick.

Layer the filling onto one half of each circle: a few slices of potato first, then a layer of onion, then a generous scatter of {{cheddar}} of cheddar. Season each layer well with salt and pepper. The filling should look well-seasoned; it will taste right once baked.

Brush the edges with beaten egg. Fold the empty half over the filling and press the edges firmly together. Lift the pasty so the sealed edge faces upward and crimp along the top in a rope pattern. Place on the lined tray, brush with egg wash, and cut a small slit in one side.
Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until the pastry is deep golden. Rest for 5 minutes before eating; the filling will be very hot.

items.symptom: Potato is still firm in the centre
items.cause: Sliced too thick, or oven ran cooler than expected.
items.fix: Slice the potato as thin as possible: aim for 2 mm. Thicker slices do not cook through in the baking time. If the pastry is browning too fast, cover loosely with foil and continue baking.
Season each layer generously before closing the pasty. The filling should taste slightly over-salted raw; the heat and pastry will balance it.

items.cause: The seam was not pressed firmly enough.
items.fix: Press the edges hard before crimping, and ensure the crimp is tight. A small amount of leakage is normal; excessive leakage means the seal failed.

items.cause: Overfilled, or the pastry was too soft.
items.fix: Use less filling and work quickly. If the pastry softens, chill the assembled pasties for 15 minutes before baking.

content.text: The cheese and onion pasty is the vegetarian version of the Cornish pasty, following the same logic: raw filling in shortcrust, crimped and baked. It is the standard filling at British bakeries and market stalls, particularly in the Midlands and the north. The combination of potato, onion, and mature cheddar works because each ingredient balances the others. The onion is sweet, the cheese is sharp, and the potato carries both without competing.
